title: Chapter 26: The Friend
content: The morning light comes through the studio window like a confession—too honest, too bright, showing every dust mote and crack in the plaster. I push through the door with my bag over my shoulder and there she is.
Suki Tanaka. Sitting in my chair. Arms crossed. Gum in her mouth, but not chewing.

That's how I know this is serious. Suki's gum is a metronome for her mood—fast when she's excited, slow when she's thinking, absent when she's furious. Still means she's at whatever comes after furious. Still means she's already decided something, and I'm about to find out what.
She doesn't say hello. She doesn't smile. She just looks at me with those dark steady eyes and says: "You're going to tell me what I saw last night."

I set my bag down on the counter. The sound it makes is too loud in the silence. Strawberry floats off her—it's the cheap gum, the kind from the bodega on Broadway, and usually I associate that smell with her laugh, with her leaning against a doorway saying something sharp and wonderful. Right now it smells like evidence.
"You saw me kiss Rowan," I say.
"I saw your skin turn into a light show." Her voice is flat. Controlled. "That wasn't kissing. That was something else."

The morning light catches the edge of the table between us, makes it glow along the grain like something alive, and I think: I could lie. I've lied before. I've lied for four hundred years. I know how to make my face do what it needs to do, how to rearrange my features into confusion, into "I don't know what you're talking about," into every deflection I've ever worn like a second skin.

But Suki is sitting there with her gum still and her arms crossed and she's not afraid. She's not running. She's demanding the truth from me like she has every right to it, and the terrifying thing is—she does. She does have every right to it. She's my friend.
I pull out the chair across from her. The legs scrape against the floor—bright, sharp, a sound like a starting pistol. I sit.
I've never told a friend before.

Lovers, yes. After they'd already seen too much—after the hum had rattled through my bones and my skin had started to go translucent and there was no more hiding. Lovers were easy in that way; desire made them willing to believe impossible things, and by the time the desire faded, the belief was already settled. But friends? Friends had no skin in the game. Friends could leave clean.

I sit across from Suki and I think about William and I think about leaving and I think about how she's already not leaving, how she came here instead, how she sat in my chair and waited for me. The studio is quiet except for the hum of the refrigerator in the corner, and I can hear my own pulse in my ears, or maybe that's the other hum, the one that never quite stops, the one that reminds me I'm always one love away from becoming someone else.
"Okay," I say. "Okay."
And then I tell her.

Not the careful version. Not the version I've rehearsed in a hundred different beds to a hundred different lovers—the one with the soft edges, the mythological framing, the "I'm something you might find in a fairy tale" bullshit that makes it easier to swallow. No. I give Suki the real one, because she didn't come here for a fairy tale. She came here for the truth.

I tell her I was born in 1623. A village in what's now the Netherlands—was then too, though the borders meant something different. I tell her I don't remember my birth name. I've had thirty-seven names since then, maybe more; some centuries I lost count, some I tried to. I tell her about the shifts—the way my body reconfigures itself, bones and skin and everything in between, how it happens when I love too much or when a century turns. The hum that builds beforehand, this low vibration in my marrow like a bell that's been struck and won't stop ringing. How it feels like dying and being born in the same breath. How each time I wake up new—new face, new shape, sometimes new gender, always new eyes looking back from whatever reflective surface I can find—and the old self is just gone, a shed skin I can barely remember wearing.

I tell her about the bodies. Not all of them—I don't have that kind of time, and neither does she—but the ones that mattered. The long jaw and broad shoulders I wore through the Enlightenment, when I was a printer's apprentice in Amsterdam and thought knowledge might save me. The small hands and round face of the Victorian era, when I was a governess named Charlotte and William was the man who called me an abomination in the same breath he used to propose. The tall androgynous frame I carried into the twentieth century, standing in a Greenwich Village bar the night the clock turned 1900 and I felt the hum begin like a train in the distance.

I tell her about the lovers I've lost. Not to death—not only to death—to time, to change, to the brutal arithmetic of a life that won't stop and a world that keeps moving. I tell her about Margaretha, who grew old while I stayed the same, who held my face in her withered hands and said, "You still look like the girl I married," and meant it as an accusation. I tell her about Aloysius, who didn't believe me until he watched me shift, and then never touched me again. I tell her about the ones who tried to understand and the ones who didn't try and the ones who left in the middle of the night with all their things in a suitcase because they couldn't bear the question of what I was.

I tell her about the loneliness. That's the part I always leave out with lovers—the size of it, the way it fills every room I've ever lived in, the way it sits beside me at every meal and follows me down every street. Four hundred years of watching everyone age and die and shift and change while I remain, or while I don't remain, while I become someone new who carries the same old hollow. I tell her how it feels to stand in a crowd of people, to be touched and held and wanted, and still be untouched in the one place that matters—known. Actually known, all the way down to the impossible thing I am.

I tell it like a confession and a eulogy at the same time. These are my sins and these are my dead and this is what I am—something that shouldn't exist, persisted across four centuries by sheer stubbornness and the inability to stop loving people who will eventually leave.
Through all of it, Suki doesn't interrupt.

Her gum stays still in her mouth. I can smell the strawberry—faint now, layered under the studio smell of turpentine and old wood—and I watch her face for the flicker that means she's about to run. The tightening around the eyes. The step backward. I've seen it so many times I could choreograph it.
I don't see it.

She just listens. Her arms stay crossed but her hands have loosened, fingers uncurling against her sleeves like she's letting go of something she was holding too tight. Her eyes stay on mine—steadier than anything, steadier than the morning light, steadier than the truth I'm giving her.

When I finish, the silence lasts long enough that I can taste the cold coffee someone left on the table yesterday. Bitter and stale and faintly sweet from cream that's gone off in the night. I stare at the cup like it might save me.
Then Suki speaks.

"Let me get this straight." Her voice is different now—not flat anymore, but careful, like she's assembling something fragile in her head. "You've been alive since the sixteen hundreds. You change bodies when you love too much or a century ends. And you've been working minimum-wage catering gigs and sleeping on a pull-out couch in Brooklyn."
Suki looks at me. Her gum finally moves—one chew, deliberate, like she's grinding something down.
"Babe." She leans forward. "What the fuck."

It's not horror. I know horror. Horror has a specific frequency—the flinch, the recoil, the way someone's entire body writes "no" before their mouth can form the word. This isn't that. This is something else entirely. This is the specific indignation of a friend who has just realized that someone she loves has been suffering unnecessarily. Her face is doing this complicated thing where anger and heartbreak are fighting for dominance and neither one is winning.

"Okay," she says. She uncrosses her arms. Puts both hands flat on the table like she's bracing herself. "Okay. So you're immortal. Kind of. And you shift. And you've been alone with this for four hundred years and you never told anyone?"

"The last person I told called me an abomination and tried to have me committed." The words come out smaller than I intend. The ghost of William hangs between us—Victorian starch, Victorian certainty, the absolute confidence of a man who looked at something he couldn't understand and decided it was monstrous. "That was 1887. I haven't told anyone since. Not until now."

Suki's face does something I've never seen before. It goes soft in a way that has nothing to do with weakness—soft like the moment before a fist clenches, soft like the intake of breath before a shout. Her eyes are bright and fierce and she says, with absolute conviction:
"William was a Victorian asshole."
She reaches across the table and takes my hand.

Her fingers are warm. Short nails, calluses from the kitchen, a faint trace of flour still white in the creases of her knuckles. She grips my hand like she's anchoring me to something—not holding me in place, just letting me know the tether is there. The touch is so simple and so specific and so entirely Suki that my throat closes around it like a fist around a coin.

"I'm your friend," she says. "You're not an abomination. You're the most extraordinary person I've ever met. And I'm pissed—" Her voice cracks, just barely, on the word. "I'm pissed that you've been carrying this alone."

I can't speak. Four hundred years and I have been fluent in every language of grief—fluent in the silence after a door closes, fluent in the particular emptiness of a bed that used to hold two people, fluent in the sound of my own voice in an empty room. But I have no language for this. For someone being angry on my behalf. For someone taking the weight and holding it up to the light and saying, "This is too heavy for one person," instead of "This is too strange for me to touch."

What do you do with that? What do you do when someone looks at the most dangerous truth you've ever told and their first instinct is to protect you from it? I've spent centuries preparing for rejection. I never prepared for this.
I grip her hand back. She lets me.
Suki stands up. The chair legs scrape against the floor again—another bright sharp sound, but this one feels different. Less like a starting pistol, more like a door opening.
She's not done.

"Okay. New rules." She holds up a finger. "You tell me when the hum gets bad. Don't wait until you're glowing like a Christmas tree. Don't handle it. Tell me." Second finger. "You don't ghost me when a shift is coming. I don't care if you need to disappear for a few days—I get it, body reconfiguration, very personal—but you send me a text. Even if it's just an emoji. I will interpret the emoji. You will not leave me wondering if you're dead." Third finger. "And if anyone—anyone—tries to hurt you over this, they deal with me first."
She cracks her gum. The sound is sharp and defiant and perfectly Suki.
"I don't have four hundred years of experience," she says. "But I have twenty-six years of being a really good friend, and that's going to have to be enough."
I laugh.

Not a polite laugh. Not a careful one. A real laugh—wet and broken and too loud for the small studio, the kind that comes from somewhere beneath the ribs where the keeping-it-together lives. It cracks out of me like ice breaking on a river, and it's not even funny, none of this is funny, but she's standing there with flour on her knuckles and strawberry gum on her breath and three rules like they're a shield she's building around me in real time, and I can't hold it anymore. The sound fills the studio and bounces off the walls and comes back to me changed—lighter, somehow. Less like something I'm surviving and more like something I'm living through.

I laugh and it sounds like crying and maybe it is both. Maybe they've always been the same thing and I just forgot. Maybe this is what it sounds like when four hundred years of silence finally finds an exit.
"Thank you," I manage. The words are too small. They're thimble-sized words trying to hold an ocean, but they're all I have.
Suki looks at me and her expression softens for real this time—not the pre-shout softness, but something gentler, something that looks almost like love.
"Don't thank me," she says. "Buy me dinner. I just had my worldview rearranged and I need ramen."
And I'm already reaching for my coat.
For the first time in four hundred years, I have a friend who knows.

Not a lover—nothing romantic to cushion it, no desire to complicate it, no reason to stay except that she wants to. A friend. Someone who looked at the truth of me and didn't flinch, didn't run, didn't try to have me committed. Someone who got angry—not at what I am, but at what I've been carrying alone.

Four hundred years of isolation just cracked. Not shattered—cracked, like the first fracture in a wall I thought was load-bearing. The weight is still there. The centuries are still there. The hum is still humming somewhere beneath my sternum, low and patient, waiting for the next time love or time will undo me.
But the weight is lighter. Just a little. Just enough.

And Suki is walking beside me toward the door, bumping my shoulder with hers, saying something about how she's going to need extra cha-shu because absorbing the existence of immortal shapeshifters is calorie-intensive, actually, and I'm laughing again—quieter now, but still real—and I think:

This is what it feels like. This is what I was afraid I'd never have. Not someone who loves me despite what I am—someone who knows what I am and stays anyway, not with desire but with choice. With stubbornness. With twenty-six years of being a really good friend and a willingness to let that be enough.
The morning light follows us out the door, bright and honest and showing everything, and for once I don't want to hide from it.
